coredata.dc:title: Purification of high–toxicity beryllium–containing water resources using carbon dot–enhanced phosphoric acid/chitosan composite gels
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Xu
Lei, Zhiwu
Hou, Hongshuai
Lin, Guanqing
Sun, Yige
Li, Haoshuai
Yang, Xipeng
Su, Yucheng
Ali, Khan Muhammad Yaruq
Hu, Eming
Wang, Hongqiang
Wang, Qingliang
Hu, Fang
coredata.dc:description: With the sensitization of carbon neutrality, the efficient removal of beryllium from beryllium-containing wastewater has gained increasing research interest in the environmental field. In this study, carbon dots (CDs)/phosphonated chitosan hydrogel (PCH@CDs) are prepared by cross-linking nanometer CDs with phosphoric acid, calcium hydroxide, and chitosan. The doping with CDs increases the number of adsorption active sites (AAS) on PCH@CDs, thereby improving the removal rates of small molecular pollutants. The adsorption isotherm results show that the maximum amount of Be(II) adsorbed by PCH@CDs in neutral water is approximately 59.84 mg/g. The excellent adsorption of Be(II) by PCH@CDs can be attributed to the homogeneous dispersion of CDs in PCH@CDs, which results in significant exposure of CDs binding sites to Be(II). In addition, the surface of PCH@CDs contains many phosphate groups, including N–H and O–H, which have strong synergistic adsorption capacity for Be(II). In simulated wastewater, the Be(II) distribution coefficient of PCH@CDs is 6.56 × 105 mL/g, which is significantly higher than that of other coexisting ions. Furthermore, PCH@CDs can reduce the Be(II) concentration to less than 5 µg/L when treating Be(II)-containing solutions with an initial Be(II) concentration of less than 5 mg/L.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Beryllium
Chitosan
Adsorption

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM analysis of PCH (a) and PCH@CDs (b), BET analysis of PCH@CDs (c) and PCH (d), FT–IR of PCH@CDs and PCH (e), XRD of PCH@CDs and PCH (f), Contact angle test of CH (g), PCH (h), and PCH@CDs (i).

(a) Influence of different adsorbents on adsorption effect (25 °C, m = 0.10 g, V = 0.05 L, t = 3h, and C0 = 10 mg/L), (b) Residual beryllium concentrations of PCH and PCH@CDs in low concentration wastewater (25 °C, m = 0.10 g, V = 0.05 L, t = 3h, and C0 = 1, 2, 3, 5, 10 and 20 mg/L), (c) Effect of pH on the zeta potential of PCH and PCH@CDs surface and their adsorption of Be(II) (25 °C, m = 0.10 g, V = 0.09 L, t = 12 h, and C0 = 10 mg/L). Final pH of PCH and PCH@CDs (d), (e) Adsorption rate of PCH and PCH@CDs for Be(II) in Be(II) solutions with different initial pH (25 °C, m = 0.10 g, V = 0.05 L, t = 3h, and C0 = 10 mg/L), Influence of co–existing ions (f) and (g).
Kinetics of Be(II) adsorption by PCH@CDs (25 °C, m = 0.1 g, V = 0.05 L, and pH = 6).
Adsorption isotherms for PCH@CDs (C0 = 10–200 mg/L, m = 0.10 g, V = 0.05 L, t = 3h, and pH = 6).
SEM mapping of PCH@CDs (a–c) and Used–PCH@CDs (d–f).

Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) of PCH@CDs and Used–PCH@CDs (a), FT–IR spectra (b) of PCH@CDs and Used–PCH@CDs. XRD analysis of PCH@CDs and Used–PCH@CDs (c). TG analysis of PCH@CDs and Used–PCH@CDs (d).
XPS analysis of PCH@CDs and Used–PCH@CDs.
PCH@CDs adsorption parameters of kinetic models.
Isotherm parameters for Be(II) adsorption by PCH@CDs.

head.ce:title: Purification of high–toxicity beryllium–containing water resources using carbon dot–enhanced phosphoric acid/chitosan composite gels

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Zhiwu Lei and Xu zhao have the same contribution.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the sensitization of carbon neutrality, the efficient removal of beryllium from beryllium-containing wastewater has gained increasing research interest in the environmental field. In this study, carbon dots (CDs)/phosphonated chitosan hydrogel (PCH@CDs) are prepared by cross-linking nanometer CDs with phosphoric acid, calcium hydroxide, and chitosan. The doping with CDs increases the number of adsorption active sites (AAS) on PCH@CDs, thereby improving the removal rates of small molecular pollutants. The adsorption isotherm results show that the maximum amount of Be(II) adsorbed by PCH@CDs in neutral water is approximately 59.84 mg/g. The excellent adsorption of Be(II) by PCH@CDs can be attributed to the homogeneous dispersion of CDs in PCH@CDs, which results in significant exposure of CDs binding sites to Be(II). In addition, the surface of PCH@CDs contains many phosphate groups, including N–H and O–H, which have strong synergistic adsorption capacity for Be(II). In simulated wastewater, the Be(II) distribution coefficient of PCH@CDs is 6.56 × 105 mL/g, which is significantly higher than that of other coexisting ions. Furthermore, PCH@CDs can reduce the Be(II) concentration to less than 5 µg/L when treating Be(II)-containing solutions with an initial Be(II) concentration of less than 5 mg/L.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Because of its unique properties, China lists beryllium (Be) as a strategic metal. Uranium–beryllium-associated deposits have been discovered in many locations, including the world’s largest deposit, which is in Xinjiang, China. With the development of mineral resources, mining activities will cause Be to migrate into soil and water environments, which severely endangers human health [1]. The World Health Organization reports that the maximum allowable Be concentration limit in wastewater discharge is 0.012 mg/L (GUIDELINES FOR DRINKING-WATER QUALITY). Studies have shown that the Be level in landfill soil is 47–50 mg/kg, which exceeds the limit by more than 20 times [2]. Considering risks to public health, an in-depth study of Be-containing wastewater is required. At present, commonly used methods for removing Be include biological, adsorption, ion-exchange, and precipitation methods. The precipitation method has a slow settling speed, and the generated precipitate and precipitating agent can easily cause secondary pollution, resulting in great difficulties for the subsequent recovery of Be [3]. At present, the adsorption capacity of biomaterials treating Be is rather small [4]. Besides, material preservation in the ion-exchange method is difficult and expensive [5]. According to the literature, the adsorption method is widely used in the treatment of Be-containing wastewater because of its economical and efficient advantages [6].

Carbon dots (CDs) are nanoscale carbon materials with low toxicity, simple synthesis, and low cost [7]. Carbon dots have a larger specific surface area than graphene and biochar [8]. CDs have been used to remove pollutants. Factors of carbon dots selectivity include its size, shape and surface functional groups. Carbon dots are hydrophobic nanomaterials with nanostructures ranging in size from 1 to 10 nm [9], which are not easily separated during wastewater treatment, so carbon dots are often used for doping materials such as gels and biological carbons [10]. Chitosan is a natural polymer that is widely found in nature [10]. The surface of chitosan contains many active groups, such as hydroxyl and amino groups [11], which can interact electrostatic with metals and are therefore widely used in wastewater treatment [12]. The surface of CDs can be functionalized with N–H, O–H, and P=O to improve the removal rate of adsorbents. For example, Perumal et al. [13] used carbon–doped hydrogel particles to remove heavy metal ions, and the results showed that the removal rate of Hg (II) reached 72 % in a single–metal ion system. Sui et al. [14] developed a nanocomposite hydrogel based on CDs and polymer–adsorbing materials that exhibited fluorescence and good mechanical properties. According to the literature, CD gels are excellent carbon adsorbents for removing inorganic pollutants from water.

Carbon-based materials are effective in removing Be(II) from wastewater. According to previous studies, Be(II) is more easily adsorbed by materials with small pore sizes [15]. Compared with biochar prepared from agricultural waste, nanoscale carbon dots (CDs) have a higher specific surface area (SSA), indicating that there may be more adsorption sites in CDs. To limit nanoscale CDs dispersion and improve adsorption site leakage into wastewater, gel materials have emerged as the best option. Gels are important adsorption materials for physically or chemically cross-linked natural polymer-adsorbing materials [16] or synthetic polymer-adsorbing materials [17]. Chitosan is widely used in wastewater treatment because of its excellent hydroxyl and amino groups.

Therefore, we hypothesize that the introduction of CDs to phospho-type gels can increase the affinity of adsorbents to beryllium and thus increase the removal rate of beryllium. We synthesized a CD/chitosan gel material (PCH@CD) modified with phosphoric acid and calcium hydroxide in this study. The adsorption isothermal model and adsorption kinetics were used to fit the adsorption process of PCH@CDs beryllium. The properties and adsorption mechanism of PCH@CDs were analyzed by means of scanning electron microscopy (SEM), SSA (Brunauer–Emmett–Teller, BET), Fourier-transform infrared (FT–IR) spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction (XRD), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Furthermore, PCH@CDs exhibit high Be(II) adsorption capacity in mixed solutions. These results show that PCH@CDs can be used to remove Be(II) from wastewater, providing a green and efficient adsorbent for treating Be-containing wastewater in mines.

Chitosan (C6nH11nNO4n, deacetylation degree ∼ 95 %), acetic acid (C2H4O2, purity: ∼99 %wt.), ammonium hydroxide solution (H5NO, purity: ∼25–28 %wt.), phosphoric acid (H3PO4, purity: ∼85 %wt.), BeO (purity: ∼99 %), and Ca(OH)2 (purity: ∼99 %) were obtained from China Shanghai McLean Biochemistry Co., Ltd.

CDs samples were prepared as described in a previous study [18]. In short, sodium hydroxide (12 g) was slowly added to 40 mL acetaldehyde and stirred for 2 h to complete the reaction. After the completion of the reaction, the product was ultrasonically treated with dilute hydrochloric acid and water until it became flocculent. The product was then washed with deionized water until it became neutral. Finally, it was placed in a 70 °C blast drying oven to dry, yielding CDs. As shown in Fig. 1 , 0.5 g calcium hydroxide was dissolved in 50 mL deionized water, and 0.5 g phosphoric acid was added at a constant rate during agitation to obtain mixed liquid A. A total of 1 g CDs was added to mixture A, and the mixture was stirred for 5 h to obtain mixture B. Mixture B was dried to obtain sample A. A total of 1 g chitosan was placed in a beaker, and a 10 mL (1 %) acetic acid solution was added under stirring until it dissolved. Further, 0.5 g dried sample A was added to the beaker, stirred with chitosan gel until a uniform solution was obtained, and left for 10 h. The phosphate-type carbon water gel (PCH@CDs) was obtained by placing mixed solids in an oven at 65 °C for 12 h. Dilute ammonia was added for 10 h, and then dried at 105 °C. The prepared sample was ground and passed through a 20-mesh sieve.

The experiment was repeated three times for each group, and the average value was obtained. PCH@CDs (m stands for dry weight g) adsorb Be(II) solution (V, L), with C0 ranging from 5 to 200 mg/L at 15 °C –35 °C. The initial pH (pHI) (2–8) of the Be(II) solution was adjusted with 0.1 M HCl and 0.1 M NH4·H2O. The Ce (mg/L) in the post-reaction solution was ensured by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry. The removal efficiency (R in %) and Qe (mg/g) were calculated using Eqs. S1 and S2, respectively.

The adsorption time (t/min) significantly affects the removal efficiency of PCH@CDs for beryllium. The qt (mg/g) of PCH@CDs for beryllium was tested under different t (0–30 min). The data were fitted using a pseudo-first-order (PFO) model (Supplementary Material Equation S3) and pseudo-second-order model (PSO) (Supplementary Material Equation S4).

The effects of various temperatures (T = 15 °C, 25 °C, and 35 °C) on the adsorption of beryllium by PCH@CDs were investigated. The Langmuir (Equation S6), Freundlich (Equation S7), and Temkin isotherm (Equation S8) models were used to fit the removal data.

The selective removal of PCH@CDs to Be(II) in simulated wastewater was investigated, and adsorption experiments were conducted in simulated wastewater containing Fe(II), Ca(II), U(VI), Zn(II), Mn(II), and Be(II). At 25 °C, 0.05 L simulated wastewater was adsorbed with 0.10 g of phosphonated chitosan (PCH) and PCH@CDs for 3 h. The distribution coefficient (Kd ) was calculated (Equation (1).
(1)
K d
=
(
C 0
-
C e
) × V
C e
× m

Fig. 2 (a) and (b) show SEM images of PCH and PCH@CDs, respectively. Fig. 2 shows that the cross-linking effect of PCH is rather poor, and the existing shapes have smaller radii than PCH@CDs. The cross-linking effect of PCH@CDs is better than that of PCH (Fig. S1), and PCH@CDs has an obvious layered structure. In addition, more abundant pores were observed on the PCH@CDs surface, which provided channels for Be(II) ions, and the chemically cross-linked CD-based gels increased the number of AAS, thereby improving the Qe of small-molecule contaminants [19].

The BET results show that the SSA of PCH is 6.0 m2/g, the average pore size is 17.4 nm, and the porosity is 0.0258cm3/g. The SSA of PCH@CDs is 1.6 m2/g, the average pore size is 14.8 nm, and the porosity is 0.0056cm3/g. The reduction in the SSA may be due to the bursting of some CDs on the material surface, which would block some of the holes. The N2 adsorption and desorption curves of PCH are shown as type III isotherms (Fig. 2 (c)), indicating that molecules gather on the surface of large pores and that the adsorption is limited. The physical adsorption curve of PCH@CDs shows a reversible type II isotherm (Fig. 2 (d)), indicating that the material exhibits unrestricted single-multilayer adsorption [20]. This can be attributed to the excellent nanoscale radius of CDs and the potential cross-linking of amino and phosphate groups on PCH, forming a dual network of CD/PCH gels. After calcification, the CD/PCH gel was soaked in ammonia to induce pore formation.

FT-IR was used to characterize the bond vibrations of PCH and PCH@CDs (Fig. 2 (e)). The peaks of PCH and PCH@CDs at 3354.0 cm−1 correspond to the OH vibration. The peak at 1411.0 cm−1 corresponds to the COOH vibration. The strong peak at 1024.0 cm−1 corresponds to the tensile vibration peak of POC. Compared with PCH, some carbon peaks of PCH@CDs were significantly enhanced. For example, the peak of PCH@CDs at 2923.0 cm−1 is a typical CH3 asymmetric stretching vibration [11]. The peak at 1550.0 cm−1 is a combination of NH deformation and C–N tensile vibration. PCH@CDs exhibited significant P–OH stretching at 890.0 cm−1 [21].

The composition diagrams of PCH and PCH@CDs were analyzed by XRD, as shown in Fig. 2 (f). PCH and PCH@CDs exhibite multiple peaks of calcium phosphate at 2θ = 25.9°, 27.1°, and 32.2°. The peaks at 2θ = 20.3° indicate the existence of chitosan. Compared with PCH, the crystallinity of chitosan is higher after CD doping, confirming that the addition of CD forms a double network of CD/PCH gels and improves the crystallinity of chitosan.

The wettability of a solid surface typically depends on its chemical composition. Fig. 2 (g–i) show the propulsive contact angles of chitosan, PCH, and PCH@CDs. The standard deviations of the contact angles of the three samples are below 5 %, which proves the accuracy of the contact angle [22]. In PCH, the lead contact angle of the material doped with phosphoric acid increases from 112.3° to 116.7°, and the hydrophobicity of the material is enhanced by calcium phosphate, consistent with previous studies [23]. In PCH@CDs, calcium phosphate may have reacted with CDs, decreasing the hydrophobicity of PCH@CDs and the lead contact angle from 116.7° to 96.0°. As shown in the SEM images in Fig. 2 (b), the porous structure of PCH@CDs surface also reduces hydrophobicity.

The adsorption effects of different PCH@CDs with different
W
H 3
P
O 4
:
W
C a
( O H )
2
:
W
CDs
ratios were studied in batch experiment. The results indicated that the removal effect of PCH@CDs on Be(II) changes with an increase of the
W
H 3
P
O 4
:
W
C a
( O H )
2
:
W
CDs
ratio (Fig. S1), the best removal rate of Be(II) is achieved at a
W
H 3
P
O 4
:
W
C a
( O H )
2
:
W
CDs
ratio of 3:3:10. Fig. S2 shows the effect of different amounts of adsorbent on removal performance. The figure shows that when the dosage of adsorbent is 1 g/L, the Be(II) adsorption rate of PCH@CDs is only 60 %. With increasing adsorbent amounts, the total number of active sites on PCH@CDs increases, and more Be(II) ions are immobilized on PCH@CDs. When the amount of PCH@CDs was 2 g/L, the maximum adsorption effect (99 %) was achieved.

Seven samples (A–G) were prepared in this study: A represents CDs, B represents chitosan, C represents CDs + chitosan, D represents CDs + chitosan + calcium hydroxide, E represents CDs + chitosan + phosphate, F represents phosphate + calcium hydroxide + chitosan, and G represents CDs + chitosan + phosphate + calcium hydroxide. When the pHI of the Be(II) solution is 6, the Be(II) removal performance of CDs is poor, with an adsorption rate of 20 % (Fig. 3 (a)). The adsorption effect of pure chitosan is also poor. When the initial beryllium concentration is 10 mg/L, the adsorption rate is only 49 %. However, the adsorption effect of the adsorbent combined with chitosan and CDs is only slightly improved (R = 63 %). Conversely, PCH slightly increases the adsorption rate of Be(II) to 96 %. When CDs are loaded, the Qe of PCH@CDs is significantly enhanced, and adsorption rate exceeds 99 %. In addition, cross-linking with chitosan addresses the shortcomings of adsorbent materials with small particle sizes, which are difficult to recover. The experimental results showed that PCH@CDs maintained a good Be(II) adsorption effect (R > 98 %) under constant conditions, demonstrating the feasibility of the PCH@CDs sample. Fig. 3 (b) shows the residual Be(II) concentrations (Ce (mg/L)) of the PCH and PCH@CDs adsorbents at low concentrations. As shown in the figure, under the same conditions, Ce after adsorption by PCH was 10 µg/L. In comparison, the C0 of the beryllium solution after removal by PCH@CDs was 2 µg/L (<12 µg/L).

Fig. 3 (c) depicts the zeta potential changes of PCH and PCH@CDs at different pH values. The zeta potential exhibites a downward trend in the pH range of 2–8. Note that both PCH and PCH@CDs are positively charged in this pH range, and their surface charges decrease with increasing pH.

The removal rate of PCH@CDs for beryllium increases as pH increases from pH 2 to pH 6, reaching a maximum value with an adsorption rate of approximately 99.3 % (Fig. 3(d)). With increasing pH, Qe remains stable. This result is due to the existence of different forms of Be(II) ions in the solution under different pHI. Be(II) is the dominant ion in solutions (pHI = 2–5.5) [24]. In addition, PCH@CDs and PCH have positive surface charges in the pH range of 2–8. The phosphate groups on PCH@CDs and the lone electron pairs on NH and PO of amino species readily form complexes with Be(II) [25]. Therefore, the removal effect of PCH@CDs for Be(II) gradually improves (pHI = 6–8). In solutions, beryllium exists primarily as positively charged Be(II). PCH@CDs have a positive charge on their surface and generate electrostatic repulsion with Be(II). PCH@CDs contain PO4 3−, OH−, and NH4 +. PO and NH in phosphate and amino groups exhibit strong coordination effects with Be(II) [25]. The surface positive charge of PCH@CDs decreases gradually in the test range, reducing the electrostatic repulsion with the positive Be(II). Therefore, starting from pH 5, the Be(II) adsorption rate of PCH@CDs gradually increases, and typically, the discharge pH of wastewater is approximately 6–7, indicating that PCH@CDs can be used to remove Be(II) from wastewater.

The mixed solution was prepared to investigate the selectivity of PCH@CDs toward Be(II) ions in the presence of other ions. The Kd (7.833 × 103 mL/g) of PCH for Be(II) was higher than that for other coexisting ions (Fig. 3 (g)). The Kd (6.56 × 105 mL/g) of PCH@CDs for Be(II) was significantly higher than that for other ions (Fig. 3 (f)). However, Fe(II) also have a higher Kd value, which may be due to the high pH after adsorption, leading to the formation of hydroxide precipitates from Fe(II). This result may be due to the presence of phosphoric acid and amino functional groups, which promote the binding of PCH@CDs to Be(II). The selective removal of PCH@CDs for Be(II) was improved. The Kd value of PCH@CDs was 83 times that of PCH, which shows that PCH@CDs exhibit selectivity to Be(II). These results indicate that the addition of CDs improves the network structure of the gel, enhancing its selective adsorption of Be(II).

The effect of removal time on the Be(II) removal performance of PCH@CDs was investigated (Fig. 4 (a) and (b)). At 25 °C, 0.1 g PCH@CDs adsorbed 0.05 L Be(II) solution at pH 6 and a concentration of 10 mg/L. Fig. 4 (a) shows that equilibrium is reached in approximately 30 min. The relevant dynamic parameters are listed in Table 1 . By comparing the R 2 values of the three kinetic models, the R2 value of PFO (R 2 = 0.986) is closer to 1 than those of PSO (R 2 = 0.952). The results show that the adsorption kinetics of Be(II) on PCH@CDs at both concentrations conform to the PFO model, indicating that the removal of Be(II) on PCH@CDs is a physical adsorption process [26,27]. In addition, the chi-square parameter (χ2) of the fitted model is lower (χ2 = 0.155). The estimated Qe values are closer to the experimental values. Due to the excellent pore structure on the surface of PCH@CDs, Be(II) is fixed on PCH@CDs.

Adsorption isotherm models were used to analyze the Be(II) removal mechanism of PCH@CDs (Table 2 ). As the T (°C) increases, the maximum Be(II) adsorption capacity of PCH@CDs increases (Fig. 5 (a) and (b) and Table 2). As shown in Table 2, the R2 value of Temkin is significantly higher than those of the other two models. Temkin also has a lower reduced Chi-sqr compared (Temkin (1.9532) < Langmuir (2.1946) < Freundlich (3.9356)) with the other two models. Those results show that Temkin can effectively fit the Be(II) adsorption curve of PCH@CDs [28]. The Temkin model shows that PCH@CDs have an uneven surface and that the adsorption process is chemisorption [29]. In summary, the theoretical Qe of PCH@CDs for beryllium at 35 °C and pH 6 is 59.84 mg/g. Compared with other adsorbents (Table 3 ), PCH@CDs exhibited superior Qe . At present, the modified chitosan resin completes the saturation adsorption of 120 mg/L beryllium in 40 min, but the pH is low, and it is not suitable for real wastewater treatment. Polystyrene-based chelating helps adsorb 1 mg/L beryllium in 30 min at pH = 8, while the material completes the adsorption of 10 mg/L beryllium in 30 min in a weak acid-neutral environment. It can be seen from Table 3 that under the same initial concentration, PCH@CDs reach adsorption equilibrium in less time. In addition, it maintains high Qe at pH 6, which shows that it is suitable for treating neutral Be(II)-containing wastewater.

SEM images of Used-PCH@CDs and PCH@CDs show that the surface of Used-PCH@CDs has no surface pores and layered structures and exhibits folds (Fig. 6 (a–e)). Fig. 6 shows that after adsorption, the O content on the surface increases, whereas the Ca content decreases, suggesting that ion-exchange and precipitation reactions may occur during the removal process. As shown in Fig. 7 (a), the observed O content on the surface of PCH@CDs after removal is consistent with the SEM mapping results, and it is speculated that an increase in the surface oxygen content is related to the precipitation of beryllium hydroxide [25].

The infrared spectra of PCH@CDs and Used-PCH@CDs are shown in Fig. 7 (b). The peak of PCH@CDs at 3354.0 cm−1 corresponds to the OH vibration. The peak of PCH@CDs at 2850.0 cm−1 is a typical asymmetric stretching vibration of CH bond from the CH3 group [11]. The peak intensification of Used-PCH@CDs at 1550.0 cm−1 indicates the existences of the NH bond and the CN bond. In Used-PCH@CDs, the peak of the OH bond is shifted from 3354cm−1 to 3279cm−1, indicating that the hydroxyl group might be complexed with Be(II). In addition, a new CP peak appears at 1473.3 cm−1, which indicates that the phosphate group might be involved in the Be(II) removal reaction [12]. In addition, after the reaction, the NH peak shifts from 2923 to 2868 cm−1, indicating that the NH bond might be participated in the reaction process. Therefore, it can be inferred that the NH bond, the OH bond, and the PO bond might be complexed with Be(II) during the adsorption of Be(II) by PCH@CDs.

The XRD analysis results of PCH@CDs and Used-PCH@CDs are presented in Fig. 7 (c). After adsorption, multiple peaks of beryllium phosphate were observed in the XRD pattern of Used-PCH@CDs; however, no peak of beryllium hydroxide was observed in the XRD pattern. Therefore, it is assumed that amorphous beryllium hydroxide is primarily formed during the reaction. These results further indicate that Be(II) is adsorbed efficiently.

Fig. 7 (d) shows thermogravimetric analysis curves of PCH@CDs and Used-PCH@CDs. PCH@CDs have three endothermic peaks, and the loss value is 0.65 % at approximately 100 °C, which is expressed as water loss of the material. The loss value between 200 °C and 400 °C is 38.53 %, which is expressed as the decomposition of the amino componds in PCH@CDs [15]. The mass loss in the third part is represented by the absence of phosphoric acid in the material [25]. Compared with PCH@CDs, the second endothermic peak of Use-PCH@CDs shift to 250 °C, indicating that the amino componds might be reacted to form a new substance during the reaction process, and it is speculated that ammonia and phosphoric acid are formed stable complexes with Be(II).

Fig. 8 shows the XPS spectra of PCH@CDs and Used-PCH@CDs, including the C 1 s, Ca 2p, Be(II) 1 s, O 1 s, N 1 s, and P2p spectra. In Fig. 8 (a), Used-PCH@CDs shows a clear Be(II) 1 s peak, indicating successful Be(II) adsorption. In the C 1 s spectrum of PCH@CDs, peaks corresponding to CO, CC, and CN are observed. After Be(II) adsorption, the CN and CO peaks shift, and their intensities increase, indicating that amino groups participate in the reaction process [34]. In the O 1 s spectrum, the peak at 531.86 eV corresponds to PO. After adsorption, the intensity of the PO peak (531.13 eV) decreases, indicating that the phosphate group participates in beryllium complexation [25]. In the Ca 2p spectrum, the vibrations at 346.32 and 350.01 eV correspond to the peaks of Ca 2p, and the peak intensity of Ca 2p decreases after adsorption. According to the zeta potential analysis of PCH@CDs, it is speculated that ion exchange occurred between Ca and Be(II) during the reaction. The P 2p peak does not change, but its intensity increases, which shows that PO is involved in the reaction. The peaks at 401.92 and 398.81 eV correspond to NH3 + and NCO, respectively [12]. In Used-PCH@CDs, the peak intensity of NCO decreases significantly, whereas the peak of NH3 + shifts and its intensity increases, indicating that the amino group has participated in Be removal. According to the Be(II) 1 s spectrum, a Be(II) 1 s peak is observed for Used-PCH@CDs, indicating that Be(II) is effectively fixed on the PCH@CDs surface.

According to the zeta potential, PCH@CDs have positive dots in the study pH range, and the adsorption efficiency increases with increasing pH, indicating a decrease in electrostatic repulsion. In addition, the characterization results showes that the surface of PCH@CDs contains PO4 3−, OH−, and NH4 +. Studies have shown that PO4 3−, OH−, and NH4 + can form stable complexes with Be(II), which enable PCH@CDs to effectively adsorb Be(II) [25]. Furthermore, strong coordination between NH and PO and Be(II) and synergistic interaction between PO4 3−, OH−, and NH4 + enhance the removal capacity of PCH@CDs for Be(II). After introducing CDs into hydrogels, they act as chemically cross-linking nodes during gelation, improving the cross-linking structure of hydrogels [35]. The addition of CDs reduces the hydrophobicity of the material, realizing its contact with Be (II) in solution. Chemically cross-linked CD-based gels increase the number of AAS, thereby improving the removal capacity for small molecular pollutants. In addition, the uniform dispersion of nano-CDs in the PCH@CDs gel matrix facilitates the binding between Be(II) and CDs. Therefore, the addition of CDs improves the Be(II) adsorption capacity of PCH@CDs [19].

To highly selectively remove Be(II) from beryllium-containing wastewater, PCH@CDs was successfully designed and synthesized. Experimental results demonstrated that Be(II) adsorption by PCH@CDs was a physical adsorption process.And the Be(II) adsorption of PCH@CDs was more consistent with the Temkin isotherm model. At 35 °C and a pH of 6, the theoretical Qemax of PCH@CDs for Be(II) reached 59.89 mg/g. In addition, PCH@CDs exhibited high selectivity for Be(II) from beryllium-containing wastewater with extremely low Be(II) concentration (<5 mg/L). The Be(II) concentration of the effluent after adsorption was below 5 µg/L, which could reachthe discharge standard from the World Health Organization reports. Mechanistic studies revealed that PCH@CDs formed by cross-linking CDs with PCH which contained phosphate, hydroxyl, and amino groups. Loading CDs into the adsorbent increased the Be(II) removal capacity of PCH@CDs at pH of 6. In summary, PCH@CDs is a highly selective Be(II) adsorbent that can be used to the deep removal of Be(II) and reduce Be(II) concentration of wastewater to discharge standards.
Not applicable

Yucheng Su: Resources. Khan Muhammad Yaruq Ali: Data curation. Eming Hu: Data curation. Hongqiang Wang: Data curation. Qingliang Wang: Project administration. Fang Hu: .
